past_medical_history: Prolonged febrile seizure at age 2 years requiring emergency department visit (seizure lasted approximately 25 minutes per parental report)
Iron deficiency anemia diagnosed at age 22, treated and resolved
Anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 24, managed with counseling
family_history: Father with history of unprovoked seizure in his twenties, never treated, no recurrence
Paternal grandmother with epilepsy, controlled on phenobarbital for decades
No family history of autoimmune disease or malignancy

social_history.occupation: Elementary school teacher, second year of teaching
social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinking, approximately once or twice per month, one to two glasses of wine
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any illicit or recreational drug use
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in an apartment, second floor with stairs
social_history.driving: Has a valid driver's license, commutes 20 minutes to school daily
social_history.exercise: Practices yoga three times per week and runs recreationally on weekends

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent with normal prosody and no word-finding difficulty. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at five minutes. Able to spell WORLD backwards correctly. Appears mildly anxious but cooperative. Approximately 8 hours post-ictal at time of examination and has returned to her baseline.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light (4 mm to 2 mm bilaterally). Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation symmetric to light touch in all divisions of CN V. Corneal reflexes intact bilaterally. Face symmetric with normal activation. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius full strength bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, and gastrocnemius. No pronator drift. No tremor or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all four extremities. No sensory extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose smooth and accurate bilaterally. Heel-to-shin without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait with symmetric arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: A small healing lateral tongue bite wound noted on the left side of the tongue, consistent with a recent seizure event. No bruising or other injuries from the convulsive event. Cardiac auscultation reveals regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.

patient.chief_complaint: Witnessed generalized convulsive seizure with preceding episodes of an unpleasant smell and nausea over the past several months
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Chanthavong is a 27-year-old left-handed female elementary school teacher who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure that occurred at home earlier today. Her partner reports that they were preparing dinner together when the patient suddenly stopped what she was doing, appeared to stare blankly ahead, and began making chewing movements with her jaw while her left hand picked repetitively at her apron. He called her name repeatedly but she did not respond. After approximately 20 seconds of this behavior, her body stiffened and she fell to the floor, followed by rhythmic jerking of all four limbs lasting roughly 60 to 90 seconds. She was briefly cyanotic around her lips during the convulsive phase. She bit her tongue during the event. There was no urinary incontinence. Afterward, she was confused and unable to speak coherently for approximately 20 minutes, then gradually became drowsy and fell asleep for about two hours before arriving at the emergency department.

On interview, the patient reports that just before the episode she perceived a strong, unpleasant smell that she describes as something like burning rubber, accompanied by a wave of nausea rising from her abdomen into her chest. She recalls nothing after that until waking up feeling exhausted and sore. She further reports that she has experienced similar episodes of perceiving a foul smell followed by nausea and a feeling of detachment approximately five to six times over the past eight months. These episodes would last 30 to 90 seconds and resolve spontaneously, leaving her feeling briefly disoriented. On two occasions, colleagues at school noticed her staring and being unresponsive for a short period, but she dismissed these as stress-related episodes. She has never previously sought medical attention for these events. She denies any recent head trauma, febrile illness, sleep deprivation beyond her normal schedule, or medication changes. She has no history of meningitis or encephalitis. She reports drinking one glass of wine the evening prior to the seizure, which is within her normal pattern.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep transients including vertex waves and sleep spindles recorded during drowsiness and stage II sleep

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging approximately 8-10 per hour during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness and light sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, dipole field extending to T4 and F4, spike duration 30-60 ms followed by 200-300 ms slow wave
findings.state: Present in wakefulness, increasing in drowsiness and NREM sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Findings indicate an epileptogenic focus in the right anterior temporal region

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 1.5-3 Hz delta activity lasting 4-10 seconds, occurring every 10-15 minutes
findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, monomorphic delta activity without evolution
findings.state: Primarily during drowsiness and early sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: TIRDA is a highly specific marker for temporal lobe epilepsy; lateralization to the right temporal region is concordant with the spike focus

artifacts.impact: Minimal, did not affect interpretation of temporal findings

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes of hyperventilation performed; mild symmetric background slowing without provocation of epileptiform discharges or seizure activity
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response at mid-range frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response elicited.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to frequent right anterior temporal spike-and-wave complexes with phase reversal at F8 and intermittent right temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). These findings are highly consistent with a right temporal lobe epileptogenic focus. No electrographic seizures were recorded during this 45-minute routine study.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording; no habitual clinical events captured during the study. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended for seizure characterization.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring for seizure capture and semiology-EEG correlation
MRI brain with dedicated epilepsy protocol (3T, thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes)
Correlate with clinical semiology and neuroimaging

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume reduced by approximately 30% compared to the left

signal_characteristics.T1: Reduced volume and loss of normal internal architecture of the right hippocampus, particularly in the body segment
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus compared to the contralateral side
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Clearly hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body and head
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute process

findings.borders: Well-defined volume loss without any associated mass or contrast enhancement

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 1.9 cm3 (below 3rd percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: Mild dilation of the right temporal horn consistent with ex vacuo change from hippocampal volume loss
No cortical malformations or areas of focal cortical dysplasia identified
No abnormal signal in the remainder of the brain parenchyma
Normal appearance of the basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum
No pathological enhancement following gadolinium administration
mri.impression: Right mesial temporal sclerosis with significant right hippocampal atrophy and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, most pronounced in the hippocampal body and head. Right temporal horn mildly prominent consistent with ex vacuo dilation. No mass lesion, acute infarct, or hemorrhage. These findings are concordant with a right temporal lobe epileptogenic focus.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low, no mass or enhancement identified
Low, no clinical history of encephalitis

recommended_actions: Correlate with EEG findings for lateralization concordance
Neuropsychological testing to assess for lateralizing memory deficits
Consider FDG-PET if pre-surgical evaluation is planned

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 80 bpm. No arrhythmia, conduction delay, or ischemic changes detected. No ECG findings to suggest a cardiac etiology for the patient's episodes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG argues against arrhythmia-related syncope as an alternative explanation for the patient's episodes.

Additional.clinical_significance: Elevated prolactin drawn approximately 50 minutes after the witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Post-ictal prolactin elevation supports an epileptic rather than psychogenic etiology.

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are within normal limits with no metabolic derangement to explain seizure activity. Prolactin is elevated at 38.7 ng/mL, consistent with post-ictal elevation following a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. TSH is normal, ruling out thyroid-related seizure etiology. Urine drug screen is negative and serum alcohol level is undetectable, excluding toxic and substance-related causes.
abnormal_values_summary: Prolactin 38.7 ng/mL (elevated, reference 4.8-23.3 ng/mL) - consistent with post-ictal state following generalized tonic-clonic seizure

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes recorded over 96 hours of continuous monitoring

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 15 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 30 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximal negativity at F8/T4 with field extending to T4 and F4, followed by after-going slow wave of 200-400 ms duration
findings.state: All states of consciousness, maximal during NREM sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Highly consistent with right anterior temporal epileptogenic zone

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric then bilateral
findings.frequency: Three habitual clinical seizures captured over 96 hours of monitoring
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta evolving to 3-4 Hz rhythmic delta over 40-70 seconds, with eventual bilateral spread. Average electrographic seizure duration 68 seconds before secondary generalization occurred in one event.
findings.state: Two seizures during wakefulness (one while reading, one while watching television), one during sleep with arousal
findings.clinical_correlation: Seizure semiology: patient reported olfactory aura (unpleasant smell) and rising nausea in the two wakeful events, followed by behavioral arrest, oral automatisms (chewing, lip smacking), and left hand fumbling. The third event progressed to focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure lasting 80 seconds. Semiology and electrographic onset are concordant with right mesial temporal lobe origin.

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs, more prominent during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Rhythmic 2-3 Hz delta activity in right temporal chain

artifacts.impact: Partially obscured the late ictal pattern during the bilateral tonic-clonic phase but seizure onset was clearly captured in all three events

output.impression: Abnormal 96-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Three habitual clinical seizures captured, all with unequivocal right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4), concordant with clinical semiology of temporal lobe seizures (olfactory aura, behavioral arrest, oral and manual automatisms). One event progressed to focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure. Frequent right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges. Findings confirm right temporal lobe epilepsy and support consideration for surgical evaluation.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during the bilateral tonic-clonic phase limited analysis of late ictal EEG in the third event.
recommended_actions: Epilepsy surgery evaluation given concordant right temporal findings
Neuropsychological evaluation for pre-surgical baseline and lateralization
FDG-PET for additional concordant lateralizing data

findings.location: Right hippocampus, most prominent in the head and body segments
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume 1.8 cm3 versus left 3.3 cm3, asymmetry ratio 0.29

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Loss of normal internal architecture of the right hippocampus with blurring of the hippocampal layers
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus on thin coronal cuts (3 mm slices perpendicular to long axis of the hippocampus), clearly abnormal compared to the left
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal head and body, unambiguously abnormal

findings.mass_effect: None. Mild ex vacuo dilation of the right temporal horn.
findings.borders: Well-defined atrophic changes without associated mass

signal_characteristics.overall: Normal signal throughout the cerebral cortex, white matter, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum bilaterally

additional_observations: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol including thin coronal FLAIR and T2-weighted images perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus
No focal cortical dysplasia identified elsewhere
Ventricular system otherwise normal for age
output.impression: High-resolution 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol confirms right mesial temporal sclerosis with marked right hippocampal atrophy and T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. No dual pathology or other structural lesions. Findings are concordant with right temporal lobe epilepsy.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Very low - no mass, no enhancement

recommended_actions: Correlate with video-EEG findings for concordance
Neuropsychological testing for lateralizing memory function and pre-surgical planning

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Below average visual memory, consistent with right (non-dominant) temporal lobe dysfunction in this left-handed patient
Impaired visual memory consolidation, lateralizing to right mesial temporal lobe dysfunction
Average verbal learning, suggesting relatively preserved left temporal lobe function

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile reveals a material-specific visual memory deficit with relative preservation of verbal memory and naming. In this left-handed patient, language lateralization may differ from typical right-handers, but the pattern of impaired visual memory with preserved verbal memory is consistent with right temporal lobe dysfunction. These findings are concordant with right hippocampal sclerosis identified on MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. The relatively preserved verbal memory suggests adequate contralateral memory reserve, a favorable indicator for surgical candidacy.
abnormal_values_summary: Visual learning (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 35 - below average, consistent with right temporal dysfunction
Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 - impaired, consistent with right hippocampal pathology

findings.location: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, amygdala) with moderately reduced uptake extending into the right lateral temporal neocortex
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.76 (normal >0.90), indicating significant right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired with concurrent EEG confirming interictal state during tracer uptake
No areas of hypermetabolism to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism, concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and right temporal epileptiform discharges on EEG. This multi-modal concordance strongly supports right temporal lobe epilepsy and is favorable for surgical candidacy.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy with mesial temporal sclerosis
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - concordant with MRI and EEG findings

recommended_actions: Multi-disciplinary epilepsy surgery conference for candidacy discussion
Consider fMRI for language lateralization given left-handedness
Discuss surgical options including anterior temporal lobectomy and selective amygdalohippocampectomy

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.value: Not applicable - patient not currently on any antiepileptic medications
Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Patient has not previously been prescribed antiepileptic medications. This is her first recognized generalized seizure, although clinical history suggests prior focal aware seizures (olfactory aura episodes) that went unrecognized.

output.interpretation: No antiepileptic drug levels obtainable as the patient was not on any AEDs prior to this presentation. Given the clear structural lesion on MRI, concordant EEG findings, and clinical history consistent with recurrent focal seizures, initiation of AED therapy is strongly recommended. Special consideration should be given to the interaction between certain AEDs and oral contraceptives, as enzyme-inducing AEDs can reduce contraceptive efficacy.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (titrate to 1000 mg twice daily over 2 weeks)
interactions: No significant pharmacokinetic interactions with sertraline
No interaction with combined oral contraceptives (levetiracetam is not enzyme-inducing)
warnings: Psychiatric adverse effects (irritability, anxiety exacerbation, mood changes) reported in approximately 10-15% of patients; monitor given pre-existing anxiety disorder
Dose adjustment required in renal impairment (not applicable with this patient's normal creatinine of 0.72 mg/dL)
May cause somnolence and fatigue, advise caution with driving (patient must be counseled on driving restrictions regardless)
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate to 200 mg daily over 8 weeks (does not interact with oral contraceptives, favorable mood profile but slower titration)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily (non-enzyme-inducing, well tolerated)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily (CAUTION: enzyme-inducing, may reduce oral contraceptive efficacy)
Zonisamide 100 mg daily, titrate to 300 mg daily (non-enzyme-inducing)

output.query: antiepileptic drug interactions oral contraceptives temporal lobe epilepsy women

results.title: Interactions between antiepileptic drugs and hormonal contraception
results.summary: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, topiramate at doses >200 mg/day, oxcarbazepine) reduce efficacy of hormonal contraceptives. Non-enzyme-inducing AEDs (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, lacosamide, zonisamide, valproate) do not significantly affect contraceptive efficacy.

results.title: Managing epilepsy in women of childbearing potential
results.source: Tomson T et al., Lancet Neurol 2019;18(2):164-176
results.summary: Valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk and should be avoided in women of childbearing age when possible. Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have the most favorable safety data in pregnancy. Pre-conception counseling and folic acid supplementation are essential.

results.title: Surgical outcomes in temporal lobe epilepsy with mesial temporal sclerosis
results.summary: Randomized trial demonstrating that temporal lobe surgery was superior to medical therapy for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy. At one year, 58% of surgical patients were free of seizures impairing awareness compared to 8% in the medical group.

output.summary: In women of childbearing age with temporal lobe epilepsy, AED selection must account for contraceptive interactions and teratogenic risk. Non-enzyme-inducing AEDs (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, lacosamide) are preferred as they do not reduce hormonal contraceptive efficacy. Valproate should be avoided given teratogenic concerns. Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have the most reassuring pregnancy safety profiles. For medically refractory cases with MTS, surgical intervention yields seizure freedom rates of 58-80%.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: Witnessed semiology is classic for temporal lobe epilepsy with olfactory aura, behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, and secondary generalization. Elevated post-ictal prolactin and unequivocal right temporal EEG abnormalities strongly argue against PNES.

No prodromal lightheadedness, pallor, or diaphoresis. Prolonged post-ictal confusion with aphasia is inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. The prolonged tonic-clonic activity (60-90 seconds) far exceeds the brief myoclonic jerks sometimes seen with convulsive syncope.

Migraine aura spreads gradually over 5-60 minutes and is typically visual or sensory. This patient's aura episodes are brief (seconds to one minute), involve olfactory hallucinations and epigastric nausea (classic temporal lobe semiology), and are followed by behavioral arrest and impaired awareness rather than headache.

Patient is 27 years old with no vascular risk factors. TIAs cause negative neurological symptoms (weakness, numbness, visual loss), not positive phenomena (olfactory hallucinations, automatisms) with altered awareness.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and witness including seizure semiology, aura characteristics, duration, and post-ictal features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Classic temporal lobe aura (olfactory hallucination, epigastric nausea) followed by behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with post-ictal confusion and aphasia
Normal interictal neurological examination with evidence of lateral tongue bite from recent seizure
Right anterior temporal spike-and-wave complexes and TIRDA consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right hippocampal sclerosis with volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, prolactin, TSH, and urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, elevated post-ictal prolactin, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to exclude cardiac arrhythmia
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy with a non-enzyme-inducing AED (levetiracetam or lamotrigine preferred given oral contraceptive use)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam or lamotrigine has no interaction with oral contraceptives; monitor psychiatric side effects given anxiety history

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions, seizure safety precautions, and contraceptive-AED interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed about state driving laws, need to avoid unsupervised swimming and heights, and importance of non-enzyme-inducing AED selection for contraceptive reliability

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for surgical evaluation given concordant structural lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral initiated for pre-surgical workup including video-EEG, neuropsychological testing, and FDG-PET

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to characterize and lateralize epileptiform activity
Obtain brain MRI to identify structural etiology (hippocampal sclerosis)
Order basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic or toxic causes of seizure
Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug therapy with attention to oral contraceptive interactions
Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety
Assess and document seizure semiology including aura to support localization
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing enzyme-inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) without addressing reduced oral contraceptive efficacy
Prescribing valproate in a woman of childbearing age given high teratogenic risk
Performing lumbar puncture without clinical indication (no signs of infection, inflammation, or subarachnoid hemorrhage)
Discharging without initiating antiepileptic therapy after a focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with clear epileptogenic lesion on MRI
Prescribing bupropion which lowers the seizure threshold
Advising that the patient can continue driving immediately
key_reasoning_points: The olfactory aura (unpleasant smell) with epigastric nausea and subsequent behavioral arrest is classic for mesial temporal lobe seizure onset, as the uncus and entorhinal cortex are common sites of seizure origin in temporal lobe epilepsy
The prior brief olfactory episodes with detachment represent focal aware seizures that were unrecognized, indicating this is recurrent epilepsy rather than a truly first seizure

History of prolonged febrile seizure in childhood (25 minutes) is a well-established risk factor for mesial temporal sclerosis and subsequent temporal lobe epilepsy, particularly when the febrile seizure is prolonged or complex

Concordance across all modalities (right temporal semiology, right temporal EEG focus, right hippocampal sclerosis on MRI) is essential for diagnosis and especially important when considering epilepsy surgery
In women of childbearing age on hormonal contraception, non-enzyme-inducing AEDs must be selected to avoid reducing contraceptive efficacy, and valproate should be avoided due to teratogenicity
Left-handedness raises the possibility of atypical language lateralization, which would be important to assess with fMRI or Wada test if surgical intervention is considered
Post-ictal prolactin elevation supports the diagnosis of an epileptic seizure; it must ideally be drawn within 10-20 minutes of the event for maximum sensitivity
